title: Confirm registration and BSN order
what: Confirm the order of registration, BRP entry and BSN in your situation.
why: EU/EEA moves often have straightforward sequencing; a quick check keeps your plan clear.
timeline: Before or in the first days after arrival.

title: Check how long you can drive with your EU/EEA licence
what: Check how long you may drive on your EU/EEA licence and when exchange or other steps apply.
why: Rules differ by origin; knowing your window avoids last-minute pressure.
timeline: Before or soon after arrival if you need to drive.

title: Plan banking and insurance in the first two weeks
what: Plan when to open a bank account and arrange health insurance after registration.
why: Starting early often makes payments and coverage smoother.
timeline: First two weeks after registration where possible.

title: Plan a long-haul arrival buffer
what: Allow extra time for jet lag, document checks and first-week admin when moving from far away.
why: Long-distance moves often need more coordination and recovery time.
timeline: First one to two weeks; avoid packing too many appointments in day one.

title: Track shipping arrival and delivery
what: Keep track of shipment status and delivery windows; have reference numbers and contact details to hand.
why: Delays and customs can affect delivery; staying informed helps you plan.
timeline: From dispatch through to delivery.

title: Keep essential documents and first-week items out of shipment
what: Carry passport, civil records, proof of address and residence documents with you — not in shipped goods. Keep first-week essentials in cabin or checked luggage.
why: You will need them for registration and first-week admin; shipments can be delayed or lost.
timeline: When packing and travelling.

title: Plan a modest arrival buffer
what: Allow a little extra time for appointments and admin when moving from a moderate distance.
why: Travel and time zones can make the first week busier; a buffer reduces stress.
timeline: First week; build slack into your plan.

title: Confirm employer onboarding documents
what: Confirm what your employer needs from you for payroll, tax and registration (e.g. BSN, bank account, ID).
why: Salary and compliance often depend on these details; aligning early avoids payment delays.
timeline: Before or in the first week after arrival.

title: Confirm salary payment account readiness
what: Ensure you know how and when you will be paid and what account or details the employer needs.
why: First salary often depends on BSN and bank account; planning avoids gaps.
timeline: First two weeks; align with registration and banking.

title: Ask your employer about the 30% ruling if relevant
what: If the 30% facility may apply to you, ask your employer how and when it will be applied and what you need to provide.
why: Eligibility and application are time-sensitive; early awareness helps.
timeline: Before or soon after starting work.

title: Confirm 30% ruling eligibility with your employer
what: Confirm with your employer whether you meet the conditions for the 30% facility and what steps are needed.
why: Conditions and application windows are strict; missing them can mean losing the benefit.
timeline: Before or soon after starting work.

title: Track 30% ruling application status
what: Keep track of the 30% facility application (who applies, which forms, expected timeline).
why: Employers often handle or support the application; knowing the status avoids delays.
timeline: Before or in the first month of employment.

title: Prioritise opening a Dutch bank account
what: Move bank account setup to the top of your list; gather documents and start the process as soon as you have BSN and proof of address.
why: Many employers and landlords need a local account; delaying can affect payments and contracts.
timeline: As soon as you have BSN and proof of address.

title: Prepare bank documents for onboarding
what: Have ID, BSN, proof of address and any employment or residency documents ready for the bank.
why: Banks often require these; having them ready speeds up account opening.
timeline: Before your bank appointment or application.

title: Check how long you can drive with your current licence
what: Confirm how long you may drive in the Netherlands on your current licence before exchange or other steps are required.
why: Periods vary by origin; knowing your deadline avoids driving without valid entitlement.
timeline: Before or soon after arrival if you need to drive.

title: Check whether you can exchange your licence
what: Find out whether you can exchange your licence for a Dutch one and what the steps and deadlines are.
why: Exchange rules depend on your country of issue; planning ahead avoids last-minute stress.
timeline: Before or soon after arrival if you plan to drive long-term.

title: Arrange a car (lease or buy) if you need one
what: If you need a car, compare options: long-term lease (operational or financial), buying new or used, or short-term rental. Many employers offer a lease car; otherwise use comparison sites or dealers.
why: Having a car sorted early avoids gaps once your foreign licence period runs out or you need one for work.
timeline: First month; align with your licence situation and work needs.
notes: Examples: employer lease, lease comparison sites, or dealers for buy/lease. Consider total cost (insurance, tax, fuel) when comparing.

title: Track which tasks must wait for a stable address
what: Identify which tasks (e.g. registration, BSN, banking) depend on having an address and plan the order.
why: Without a fixed address, some steps are delayed; knowing the sequence reduces confusion.
timeline: Before or in the first days after arrival.

title: Review childcare, school and family registration follow-up
what: Plan any extra admin for family members: registration, IDs, school or childcare, healthcare.
why: Family moves often involve more documents and appointments; planning avoids overload.
timeline: First month; align with your own registration and key steps.

title: Bring South Africa–issued official records if registration or family admin may need them
what: If municipality, IND or family registration may require South African civil records (e.g. birth, marriage), ensure you have them to hand when you arrive.
why: Having the right documents ready at arrival avoids delays in registration or family admin.
timeline: When travelling; keep with your hand luggage.

title: Check South African driving licence rules in the Netherlands if you plan to drive
what: Check RDW rules for using or exchanging a South African driving licence in the Netherlands.
why: Deadlines and conditions differ by country; plan ahead if you need to drive.
timeline: Before or soon after arrival.

what: Use the dedicated guide for moving from South Africa to the Netherlands for country-specific tips.
